Good professor. Not the best at answering questions, but lecture was clear and basic examples were given. Exams were very fair and his personality was funny. His lecture style is very foundational. Do the problem sets or you will have a hard time.
When he responds to emails, he is really informal and really dry response. I personally, hate his class the most in my entire life as a student. Not worth even a one star.

His lectures are filled to the brim with jokes and yet he still somehow manages to teach everything properly. If you have time, you should definitely attend his lectures in person. This was his last semester teaching EECS 16A, but rest assured that you will enjoy any class he teaches. He's also quite helpful and supportive in office hours!
All he does is flirt with his other teaching professor and get mad at students.

First, he cares deeply about his students, and attending his lecture is pretty fun. Although sometimes his lecture style is too slow or a little bit boring, he generally has a pretty solid lecture style.

Listen, he's a really funny guy and I think he's engaging but the way 16A is designed absolutely sucks. If you can somehow get out of it (take 54 or maybe CC), do it. 16A mashes together too many disparate concepts and there's way too many different types of work going on every week. Screw the idiot who designed the 16 series, but Miki's nice tho.

I cannot comment on Professor Lustig's teaching since I took this class before this year, but in terms of logistics, this class has too much going on between the self-grades, the resubmissions, et cetera. This class reminds me of a chef who cooks 3 soups of the day instead of ONE soup of the day. Sometimes, less is more.

He's a humorous professor, but his lectures move pretty fast and he sometimes fails to explain ideas clearly. The exams are also a mess as they did nothing to prepare for the shift from in-person to online format. Apparently having more questions on an exam is, to them, a great way to compensate the failure to provide partial credit. Horrible exam.
The tests are long and they did not do anything to prepare you for the new format during the pandemic. His lectures are unclear at times. But I love his jokes during lectures.

His lectures are super entertaining and quite helpful to learn the material even if you end up with some gaps in your knowledge. 16A with him is not easy, but if you put in the proper work it is totally doable and very interesting. Definitely would recommend.
Prof is enthusiastic about lectures. He will keep you laughing and entertained while you probably fail his course. I think his teaching is an average but great personality.

I just love Miki's enthusiasm for lectures. He always gives a big woohoo at the start of lectures to light it up. His lectures are great and well-prepared. He has slides for every lecture, so it's easy to go back and review the contents. Miki is also a hilarious prof who inserts jokes here and there to keep everyone focused during online lectures.

He loves his work and shares that excitement in his lectures, which are interesting and funny. He can sometimes go a little too slow or repeat himself a lot in lecture, but overall they are informative and clear. Projects and homework are time-consuming and sometimes frustrating, but a good experience that teaches you CS.

Heaviest-workload class Ive taken at Cal (makes EE 126/105 look chill) but WORTH IT. Labs/hws are long but clearly useful/very cool. Tests arent straightforward but theyre open-book/note and not too long, so theyre not stressful. Very understanding (wont hit you with a long hw after an exam) and REALLY loves teaching the material, and it shows

Prof. Lustig is perhaps the most underrated prof at Berkeley. He spends hours preparing lectures and takes very careful time to explain every concept and walks through problems with the class. Nobody shows up for class though most of the time. Compared to other ee16b teachers, Lustig is amazing. Prepare for some theory heavy exams though.
One of the most passionate lecturers I have ever had. You can truly care he loves the material, and it makes a world of difference as a student. Awesome professor, awesome person.
i love how well prepared he was for each class.

Taking this class severely damaged my GPA. A grading scale where the final is 65% and homework is 0% is simply absurd. It puts students at a massive disadvantage, especially since taking this course with other professors guarantees a much fairer and lenient grading structure. Do yourself a favor and stay away from this professor.

The grading policies in this class were absolutely horrendous. 4, 20 minute quizzes, each worth 5% of your total grade (every section had different quizzes too), you either know it or you don't, and if you dont then be prepared to be knocked one half letter down for final grade. No drops, no midterm, one final, 65% of grade. 1 in 5 students failed
Great lecturer, cares a lot but I've never seen a more horrid syllabus in my life.

One of the best lecturers that I have had at Berkeley. Exams are not difficult if you have watched all the lectures and done the HWs. She genuinely cares about making sure students understand and is very accessible on Edstem as well. If you haven't understood the HWs, you won't be able to derive the linear algebra frqs on the exams.

Prof Ranade's lectures were pretty good, if slightly boring. Her homeworks are exams are killer though, definitely need to prep a lot for them. She is accessible during OH and the staff are helpful though.

Compelling lecturer, great person. But calling on people at random, presumably to see if they are paying attention, in lecture is immature and giving tests that are meant to be taken over the course of 110 minutes in an 80 minute section doesn't make a whole lot of sense.

She is very good at explaining the material and and she is able to answer every student's question with clarity. The discussions are helpful and if you go to lecture and do the homework, the exams are fairly straight forward. My favorite professor so far!!

Early in the course, I found myself disliking professor Ranade. I thought her class was too hard and she didn't run it well. However, being honest, my poor performance was often due to my own lack of effort into the course. I'll admit, the course is challenging, but if you do everything that is asked of you, there is nothing hard about it.
Prof.Ranade's 127 is great. But get ready to work hard as she is like your big sister who expects you to go grad school

Took Ranade for 16A and B, loved her for both. 16B was significantly harder for me, but I was a lot less prepared for it. Go to lectures and take good notes. Discussion helps a ton. She has super organized lectures and exams that are patterned after the homework and discussion.

I LOVED Ranade's lectures. Super well organized, and she was willing to stop the class multiple times to go over confusing parts again. The class gives you so many opportunities to learn the material, from lecture to discussion to homework. However it is challenging, especially for someone new to circuits. The bin shift at the end was great, too

She knows several students who took 16A last semester and she was able to build upon concepts from 16A especially matrices. After an exam, she would have one-on-one talks. Many of the TAs are helpful during homework parties and office hours. The website has a clear grading scheme. This semester, it was lenient because of the pandemic.

Even though the course was a lot more chaotic being online during the pandemic, prof. Ranade really made it a lot easier on us with the staff review sessions before the final, the binshifts, the availability of course staff members for 1-on-1's and check-ins, etc. You can tell she genuinely cares about the students and wants us to do well.

After a year of being forced to take 16A/B, I now regret transferring to UCB. I have no respect for Sahai and Ranade--they force these classes on students to serve themselves. They disrespect circuit analysis to hide what 16A/B really is--a poorly taught intro to ML. At least 90% hate 16A/B, and have no use for it. Wasted two upper divs for me.

Prof. Ranade is very smart and knows her field, but her lectures are hard to follow at times especially in 16b so go to office hours. The GSI's that taught the discussions are life savers though. HW is REALLY long and sometimes doesn't seem to help.

Professor Ranade's lectures were the best thing about this class (which isn't saying much because this is the worst class I've taken in my entire life). Her lectures are clear and actually helped me understand the concepts more. However, the homework always takes so long (around 10 hours if you do the whole thing) and isn't that useful.
Took this class Spring 2021 and it is difficult class for no reason. Very disorganized and the TAs don't know how to handle the course

I took this class my first semester at Cal (Fall 2020) remotely. She gives interesting lectures and test was reasonably easy (very similar to homework). I have to say that 90% of the learning was done through homework and reading the notes. Didn't attend discussion and watched all lectures at 2 times speed. Make sure you understand every note well.

Professor Ranade explained things really clearly. She would always take the time to ask the class how we were feeling with the material and she thoroughly answered questions. Though I did not particularly love EECS16A, I also appreciated how excited Ranande got about the material, or the "magic." Her passion comes through. Exams are hard though.

I don't get why some people are being so negative. I took EECS16A this fall and I thoroughly enjoyed it. The course staff was super helpful on piazza, and the discussion sections were very useful. The class was pretty challenging but at the same time it wasn't overwhelming. I thought prof. Ranade did a great job!
Her class this semester was very time consuming with the homework and preparing for exams. Other than Moses I did not find any resources very helpful.

She is great at explaining concepts by drawing diagrams of circuits and other important concepts. She holds office hours after class and calms down students after the first midterm. Although I didn't have a real in person lab experience, I enjoyed building circuits at home and the software component. Homework and tests are long, but doable.

EECS 16A was pretty useless but Ranade tried to make it better by having office hours and virtual things. I just found this class very disorganized. There were just class notes outside her lectures and some useless textbook some clueless TA wrote which didn't help at all.

Her EECS16A class is a big time commitment. Labs, homework, lectures; all take a solid amount of time. Prof. Ranade seems fully aware of this and every aspect of her class is run really well. She makes sure to really drill in the most important concepts during lecture. Pretty fair grader too. Looking forward to EECS 16B.

Professor Ranade is a really passionate and intelligent educator. Her lectures are very good at explaining concepts and breaking them down into easy to understand parts. She will draw attention to the more important concepts but you should take time to pay attention to lectures and readings or you will feel behind. Homework if hard but important.

The most boring/useless class I've ever taken. The HW/exams are long/tedious and notes/lectures are poor/confusing. She does not write the exams, and the GSI's don't write well-structured exams. They are too many problems, and each question is a one-page essay written for children. Other past exams from other Prof.'s were much more concise.

Her course is structured well, she communicates very clearly (expectations, grades, what to know, etc), and is understanding of students. Her lectures aren't the best as she often takes a long time to explain one concept, but all-in-all the course is a tough, but you'll come out having learnt a lot.

Gireeja is one of the most caring professors I have met at Berkeley. However, she ought to prepare more for her lectures cause she does make quite a few mistakes that can be distracting and derail lecture for several minutes. I also wish she would be more transparent with her grading scheme.
Crystal clear lectures, fair tests, the right amount of homework. Overall a great class and she does a great job of teaching it.

Ranade is super eloquent, clear, and concise. She knows what she's talking about and is always ready to answer peoples questions. I think she's also encouraging. Sometimes lectures would get pretty boring, and I would recommend watching webcast if you prefer fast paced, as she speaks slowly (probably because this class is meant for freshmen).
Amazing and very clear lectures! Prof Ranade is willing to help us outside of the class. She makes ee16a clear and easy for understanding.

Professor Ranade isn't the best lecturer (ironic, since she basically wrote the content of the course). Going in with no linear algebra or circuits experience was tough, but asking a TON of questions in discussion, reading supplemental texts and lecture notes, doing homework diligently, and going to office hours helped me succeed in this course.

I learned more about linear algebra and circuits from the notes and the optional texts assigned for the course. Going to lecture is absolutely worthless and the homework is unnecessarily long. You can get perfect scores on all the homework assignments but it all comes down to your performance on tests which are quite lengthy.

Lectures are clear, tests are fair, homework is difficult. There is clearly a lot of thought and preparation that goes into each assignment which is nice because they are not easy assignments. Very rarely feel like you're doing busywork.

Most professors in college just regard teaching as job, but don't really care, while professor Ranade is one of the very few who actually cares about how students are doing and really want to make sure that her students are learn something. Perhaps a normal lecturer, but amazing teacher or educator. Remind me of my high school teachers.

Disproportionate amount of work combined with her rushed teaching style makes it hard to learn. Theres hw due on Fridays, self-grades due on Tuesdays, 3 hour lab, and two discussion sections, repeating every week. If you have no prior knowledge of linear algebra, you'll have to spend extra time outside of lecture going over concepts.
Not beginner-friendly, was difficult to understand her explanation of linear algebra

Lecture is boring, dull, and confusing. She also doesn't let you take notes on a tablet/laptop which is annoying since I do that for all my other classes. Hard to understand her mathematical notation and intuition if you have no linear algebra prior experience.

Not recommended. Professor Rao's teaching style was difficult to follow, and he did not feel supportive when students needed help progressing in the course. Students should be cautious and clearly understand all course policies, as academic integrity concerns are taken very seriously with impersonations.
Super hard exams. Not bad of a guy (quite funny outside of class), but his teaching style is terrible. Made exams super difficult compared to past sem. But overall, meh

I really appreciate Rao's emphasis on intuitive understanding of content and his approach to learning. Contrary to what others say, I thought the lectures were actually pretty useful in solidifying understanding on top of notes. Tests were hard, but grading was fair and TAs were quite responsive.

This class is all learn the material by yourself. Don't even try to understand his lecture, since he just read the slide without explanation and asking you "right?". Suggest learn the probability part from youtube.

Class went over tons of topics really fast which is the typical recipe for a highly challenging course, will require lots of diligence and huge variation in difficulty based on previous experience which is unfortunate, still can learn a lot and generally well designed, prof himself is OK but notes/discussions/hw are the key to learning
be aware he seems to be making exams significantly harder which is wild for an already infamous class

Professor Rao is a great and caring person who genuinely cares about his students. I think most people find his class hard because they do not read the lecture notes ahead of time, which is something he assumes all of his students do. If you read the notes ahead of time and try your best to understand them, then you will succeed in his class.

I don't get it why he makes the exam so many questions and no one can finish. Reading notes before attending classes does help but not too much and usually it takes hours to finish reading. His lectures are just reading out the slides with no explanation.

Worst professor on campus. Is condescending to students and unwilling to help. Lectures are redundant. The only way to pass this class is by watching videos of someone else's teaching. Avoid at all cost.
This professor can't teach. Although he's somewhat comedic, he makes the content harder to understand. So at the end of the day, you're just teaching yourself discrete mathematics.

Rao's teaching style can be hit or miss. Lectures are tough, but the material is manageable with the free textbook and helpful resources like unstuck study. Grit through the challenging assignments, and you'll learn a lot.

Please stay away if you can. He is demeaning toward students and tries to antagonize. He has actually falsified his identity in a bate and switch / catfish scheme to push students to admitting things that are not true. He also does not follow university teaching policy. Very scary, yells a lot, makes sexual comments about female students.
Come to lectures having read the notes beforehand, and his lectures will make a lot more sense.
Best professor I had at Berkeley.
Hard to understand

Entertaining lectures, but they're incomprehensible. No clear grading policies and no clear resources to learn. Pretty much just him yapping throughout class and expecting us to pick up something while he's putting no active effort into creating a good learning environment
Great guy but can't teach at all. I was much better off not coming to class.

The class would have survived without him. Lectures were beyond useless as he always told us to just watch DeNero's videos, which you will have to do anyways because he is a terrible lecturer. DeNero is the only reason this class is as organized as it is.

I think Rao is a great guy, but he just can't teach. His explanations are not intuitive and he reads off of slides while skimming through proofs. You will end up relying on discussions/OH/notes to learn the content. His vitamins often have typos, and his problem sets are very hard. Thankfully, his exams are heavily curved.

Professor Rao is an amazing human being first and foremost. Perhaps he may not be the best at lecturing, but whatever lack in that area he has, he makes up for in humaneness and ingenuity. Rao equipped me well to deal with the hurdles that many computer scientists will come to face in their careers as breakthrough artists of technology.

Apathetic but in a good way. Didn't want to teach the class so can't hold much against him. Discussion of programming as a religion is always interesting, and the way he talks about linked lists really calms my nerves. In a word: Overhated.

Started the first 4 lectures of cs170 without our knowledge. Made this the hardest start to a class ever and caused me 3 missed homeworks because I can't even understand the prerequesite knowledge he's "teaching". If you get him, watch 2022's lectures.

Funny guy but his lecturing is some of the worst I've ever seen. I can't understand a single thing this guy teaches in 1.5 hours, and a 20 minute youtube video will explain everything I needed. For cs70, he assumes you've read notes and understood them before coming to lecture, so he can recap (he doesn't help).
Wonderful guy, fun lecturer.
I do not know why he shows up for CS170 lectures and the lectures he was given are not good.
Very bad lectures
I don't like his style.
Homework hard af
Doesn't really care about students.
Weird class type
Very funny jokes
Idk what to say
Denero's better

Prof. Rao leans heavily on personal anecdotes, especially about his daughter, rather than focusing on course material. Concerns arise about his depth of knowledge in the subject. Interactions with students often stray into personal matters, raising questions about maintaining a professional and ethical teacher-student relationship.

He doesn't care, and 70's design generally reflects that. The notes have a lot of gaps which means sitting for hours trying to figure out what's going on, when most of the material isn't that complicated = artificial difficulty.

Genuinely a good bloke. People joke about Rao but he's got a heart and he really does care. He went out of his way to make himself accessible to us students (he got us boba!). Rao doesn't lecture like a Youtuber, he lectures like a human. He treats us like humans. Sure he's got his flaws, but I wouldn't have preferred to take 61A with anyone else.
Slow with the tech and not efficient with time but he provides a strong way of thinking regarding programming Woohoooo

Just gave the starting lecture for 170 and I genuinely could not understand what he was talking about or why. Reminded me of the time I took 70, it was painful. He is just a bad lecturer, cannot follow his thoughts at all. Just his lecture style made it difficult to learn and understand.

Lectures in this class felt redundant, serving as mere summaries for those who had already read the notes. The course seemed more like a do-it-yourself experience, where reading the notes several hundred times was required to fully understand the concepts.Extremely difficult exams.

DON'T TAKE HIS CLASS! The professor uses resources and materials from a professor "John Denero". I have had bad experiences understanding his lectures and the example he used wasn't clear. He isn't horrible since he gives enthusiasm and energy to his lecture, but his lecture didn't help me prepare for CS assignments/tests.

I found that understanding the material in Professor Rao's class was challenging. It seemed that there were gaps in the explanations provided, and I believe a more thorough grasp of the subject matter would improve the overall learning experience for students. Constructive feedback and additional clarification could benefit the class.

CS70 with Professor Rao and Tal was a life-changing experience and deeply shifted my perspective on how I interact with difficult concepts. Rao lectures are concise and intuitive - he makes sure you see the forest for the trees. CS70 is transformative: me going from a 3-week-behind desperate student to enjoying proofs is thanks to Professor Rao.

Rao is a great person and an incredible researcher. However, if you do not have math competition(I'm talking about a decent rank in AIME) experience, DO NOT take CS70 without understanding of discrete math. His lecturing was awful. The notes are massive amount of text. Rare resources. Literally no one goes to lec. EXTREMELY difficult exams. RUN
He does not teach.

Spring 2023 here. Rao is an amazing lecturer, his jokes are hilarious and keep you engaged. The only people that find him bad or rate him terribly are people that don't understand his lectures, people that don't pay attention, don't read the notes, and don't know how to learn in general. I actually prefer him over Denero and Hug (both were great).

I took this class over the summer when Rao didnt teach it (it was taught by 3 students who actually knew how to teach and wanted us to succeed). I STRONGLY URGE & ENCOURAGE anyone to take this course during the summer without Rao. Even though it's a math class, the staff and instructors ensure that you understand the material and want you to pass.

During the Spring 2023 semester, Professor Rao's CS70 had a heavy workload that exceeded expectations. His lectures lacked engagement, mainly reading off slides, and he struggled to answer questions effectively. Exams were disproportionately difficult, testing topics barely covered. Overall, a disappointing learning experience.

I only went to the first 4 lectures or so and a few more online but I liked his teaching style. Also the exams were fun, loved doing the proofs. The midterm should've had more time because it was a race. The final was well paced. The >73% equals full credit for HW is the best policy bc HW is for practice, not to punish you for missing minor things.

Seemed like a chill dude. Lectured heavily off slides. Cracked jokes and didn't put in a ton of effort. Wasn't the best explainer and wasn't very engaging. Made concepts more challenging than they needed to be. Probably a nice person but was definitely phoning it in.
Excellent professor, wonderful lectures, jokes can vary, but gets the essence of the content across quite well.
You either love him or you hate him. I certainly loved his teaching style and his sense of humour.
Horrible lectures...he basically just reads slides off the screen and is quite boring. I'm glad he only taught discrete math instead of probability or I would suffer more.
Horrible, definitely need a tutor for this class. Worst professor Ive had.

Although Prof. Rao's lecture is fast-paced, I really like his teaching style. His lecture is so hard that you have to read notes multiple times. If you can understand ALL concepts in his lecture slide, you are able to solve most exam-level questions. Overall a very positive experience.

CS70 is a tough but Rao is horrendous (data: massive # poor reviews cf to other UCB CS70 profs) - error laden slides / mumbles explanations / exams are heavily trick questions / hw impossible and encourages cheating. Worst aspect: uses the classic "you need to learn the material" to obfuscate his lack of motivation and clarity in teaching.
He has no idea how to present the content of the class. Horrible at teaching, ridiculous exams, and his TAs know nothing about the content. Worst professor in CS department

Prior to the class, a student told me, "don't waste your time going to Rao's lectures." Now, I can see why. His slides look like a bunch of random facts, and all he does is read them without any adequate explanation. Too bad for you if you aren't a math-cracked kid. Miserable but Indian gurus from YouTube will be your best friends for the semester.

I've never had a professor explain things more poorly than professor rao. Watching one 90 minute recording of his videos takes me well over 3 hours because he explains things so horribly and glosses over everything. I constantly find myself watching youtube videos to find proper explanations. At least he is funny.

CS70 was by far the most challenging class I've ever taken at Berkeley. The spring 2022 final was absolutely atrocious and significantly harder than previous years. Even with attending both discussions, both CSM sections, both lectures, and office hours 5 days a week spending 10-15 hours on homework per week, barely any of it helped for the final.

Pros: - Lenient and cool guy - Curved (everyone bombs the tests) Cons: - class has very little support and it doesn't feel like there are resources for your success - Homework's are extremely difficult - Discussions are not recorded - Solutions for tests are not clear - Lectures are 100% useless and terrible
Rao is legit one of the best professors at Cal. Nice guy, very accommodating, brilliant at explaining things. CS70 is a hard class but Rao really does right by it.
Hard class and content. Rao is caring tho. Bumped up my grade to the next bin because I was so close��. A pretty decent guy in my book.
This class was hard AF. Prof Rao's lectures and notes were useful for understanding the material.

Very nice person but lectures are fast and rely too much on (kinda confusing) slides. Lecture content similar to the notes. Read notes before coming to lecture. Accessible in OH and Piazza. Exams w/ many short answers & 50% averages. I enjoyed the short answer format because you don't have to come up with algorithms and whatnot unlike other profs.

CS70 is the hardest class I have taken so far. Professor Rao's lectures are very confusing if you do not have a baseline understanding of the material, but are helpful if you do. Personally, they were the most useful to me after doing the discussions and homeworks. He is a funny and nice person, and I can personally imitate his woohoo quite well.

His lectures can be confusing, but that's because he explores difficult problems that you have to look back at and think about. I found the content of his lectures extremely interesting, although his presentation was a bit dry. Some of the homework questions were ridiculously hard, but most were a good level of challenging and interesting.
This class is ridiculously hard and has unclear grading policies. Homeworks are very long (i spent 10+ hours a week) meanwhile they won't even grade every question. Rao's lectures are not helpful
Very unclear lectures, not easy to follow on the already hard material

I was ROBBED of an A- when he refused to give me an A- when clearly, I am at the border of an A-. I wish he gave some compensation for the Zoom issues that happened during the exams. He refuses to give out the grading bins so that students can ensure that grades are calculated correctly and fairly. This class is the hardest one I have ever taken.

This man should not be allowed to be a Professor, he does not know how to teach. His "lectures" consist of quickly reading off badly formatted slides--completely lazy and useless. The exams are absurdly long, and riddled with typos and errors. Avoid this Professor if possible, because it's probably a much more rewarding class with someone else.

Fall 2020: Doable, but make sure you have a solid background in discrete math or a lot of time to devote/an easy schedule. Class is graded on a curve so your performance is based on your standard deviation from the class mean/z-score. I ended up spending 6+ hrs/week aside from lectures, but some people who got higher grades spent a lot more.

Exams and homework filled with typos, very vague grading policies. You knew how you were performing relative to the mean on exams, but he gave no estimate of where that put you grade-wise. Discussions were not recorded and varied heavily, so it was luck of the draw for the quality of discussion, as you could not watch others. Terrible course.

Not the best lecturer, but definitely one of the most caring and compassionate professors I've had at Cal. He was flexible with course policies. Notes are almost more important than the lectures, and exams are rather difficult though.

Professor Rao seems like a nice person, but personally, I had trouble with the pacing of the class. The course was really fast-paced for me, homework took 10+ hours each week, and exam averages were 50%. Lectures seem like a hit or miss for some people, but reading/understanding the class notes is crucial if you want to pass the class.

The class started out not too bad, lectures aren't helpful but he was at least entertaining in a weird way. The homework was long, difficult, and often had typos/mistakes in the questions. On top of the difficult content, prof was unhelpful and vague, esp with logistical questions about grading or the class.

His lectures were extremely confusing, halfway through the class I gave up and just read the text on my own. He fails at reading the slides for a solid amount of lecture time. His grading policies are extremely unclear and the class seems extremely poorly ran. His only response is "master the material". Which isn't very helpful. Avoid at all costs.
In my experience he's pretty unremarkable, not great but not awful either.
if you take a class with him after reading these or talking to anyone who took a class with him, your loss of mental well-being and grades, as well as dislike for the subject is only your own fault

Nice and caring guy, but reuses Sahai's infamous hw's or writes his own which are filled with typos and missing needed info to answer them (doesnt update pdf). Gives exams with reused true/false questions since hes lazy. Worst lecturer of all time (literally reads from the slides), the best advice I can give to anyone is avoid him at all costs.

fall20 He may be knowledgable as a person, but a horrible lecturer. Not only that, but he's not willing to listen to students and to any inconvenience or unfair policies, his answer is "just master the subject." I suggest students to teach themselves all discrete math before taking him. I had to use youtube and other universities' notes to study.

Terrible professor who doesn't understand student issues. Non sympathetic and causes high levels of emotional distress. I strongly recommend you avoid this professor at all cost, for he isn't a good lecturer, nor does he provide any growth to your academic career.
The more he tries to explain something the more confusing it gets. Just read the textbook instead.
Best CS professor at Cal. Took both CS 70 and CS 170 with him. His lectures are very clear so that textbook is not needed. Exams are fair. Would probably take every course Rao teaches in the future!

Rao is likely the worse professor at UC Berkeley. I was expecting way more quality, especially since this Cal is regarded as one of the top institutions in computer science. He might be smart, but he does not know how to teach. Students seem to rely mostly on notes/textbook to survive. He relies on cheese jokes to gain some respect.
Horrible lecturer. Did not understand a thing. If you have the choice, avoid taking a class with Rao at Berkeley.
Very bad lecturer. Usually did not get anything out of lecture. He reads off of his 100+ slides at a rapid pace. However, I can tell that he cares about his students and want us to succeed.

Terrible Lecturer, amazing in office hours. You can read others' opinions on his lecturing, but the few times I did go to office hours - he's amazing. If you have him and are struggling, go to his office hours. He's great when he's not on the podium.

Very poor lecturer. You can tell he is trying and has read the literature, but the way he speaks and delivers material is disjointed and confusing. Also was not amazing at writing test questions. He usually did a pretty good job, but when he made mistakes or wrote poorly worded questions, he refused to allow other valid answers.
He is really bad at giving lectures. Really smart guy.

A genuinely nice guy and you can tell he is a genius, but if you are not on his level don't expect to understand a single thing he explains. He is a horrible lecturer who doesn't know how to put what he knows into words, and assumes everyone knows everything. Honestly he should just focus on research and give up teaching.

I took CS70 over the summer, but the lecturers were quite bad so I watched Rao's webcasts on double speed to learn the material. His lectures were quite funny (terrible jokes but he's a cool guy so it was funny). They were a little scattered and fast for some students, but I really enjoyed them. Would def take a class with him in the future!

Good attitude, but painfully awkward jokes and awful at teaching. It's obvious that Rao gives lecture on the fly by literally reading every word off of the slides and getting lost during his own lectures. Teaching is his last priority, so if you take it with Rao don't even go to lecture and just read Sinho/Alvin's notes.

One of my favorite professors, because he genuinely cares about his students. I learned discrete math really deeply and it prepared me well for cs170. Really glad I got to take a class from someone that is brilliant but also compassionate

Nice guy, but lectures are sometimes unclear (he skips a few important steps in math proofs). So, you'll probably have to spend extra time reading the course notes. That being said, he is really nice and somewhat helpful during OH. Exams for this class are tough, but he ends up curving nicely. HW was tough, but 80%+full credit was nice. Tests are tough
Satish is pretty nice and approachable but his lectures are indeed really scattered. The curve is nice though

It is interesting how UC Berkeley has such accredited professors however they are clearly not known for their teaching experience. Professor Rao is a nice, caring and smart but notoriously bad teacher teaching very hard material. In a hard class like this precision in speech is important, which Professor Rao himself confesses to be horrible at.

I've taken both 70 and 270 with him and I've also TAed for him. He's really a sweetheart that deeply cares about his students and being fair. He's a little scatterbrained but ask him questions if you're unclear about something---you'll understand more and he'll slow down. Also, read the notes! (For 70)

Rao explains concepts very well and gives very good examples to strength understanding. Tests are hard but reasonable. Offers test-only option which relieves the stress of the course. Go to lecture, go to discussion, skim through homework, and do some practice exams, and you'll do well.

Rao was genuinely a good person. He was incredibly good at explaining concepts at office hours but his lectures were a little scattered. It was a must to read through the course notes and do a lot of practice just to get lecture to make sense to me. Overall he coherently explained the material (once it already made some sense) and he graded fair.

The worst class I've ever taken at this school so far. Not that I don't find the topic interesting, but it's so hard to keep up when the lectures are so horrible. Rao is very knowledgeable of the content, but terrible at teaching it. I hate asking for help, so I never went to office hours, and I was confused the entire semester as a result.
Really great. Really caring.
Love him! Really good at explaining concepts. Has a unique lecturing style (as in makes a lot of stupid jokes) but that helped me not get bored. One of the best math/science professors there is.

Rao is SO CONFUSING in lecture!! He is all over the place and never coherently finishes any of his sentences when he talks. He just is just way too hard to follow. Concepts like these need clear instruction from the teacher!!

Rao's a nice guy, can be humorous & chill. He isn't the greatest instructor though. His lectures lack so much clarity. He has typos and makes mistakes while lecturing/explaining things - confusing. He's nice and willing to help if you go to his office hours though. But overall do not recommend for a course like this. You need a good clear teacher.
Satish is amazing, extremely helpful and devoted and has an awesome personality

This is a difficult course and Rao is not a good professor to teach it. He's scattered in his lectures, goes way too fast through the slides, and he assumes that you know various math concepts before starting the course. If you're comfortable with solving math proofs, you'll do well in this class. If not, take it with another professor if you can.

Rao is a poor lecturer - he can't seem to clearly explain things. He trails off easily and is extremely hard to follow in the first place. Basically, he is all over the place during his proofs and explanations, sometimes skipping essential details altogether. For a class that is so theory-heavy, it sucks a lot to have such a bad instructor.

A scatter-brained lecturer. He is smart but he seems to have a difficult time conveying his thought process and concepts to the class. He will jump around a lot during his math proofs. He will skip steps, goes too fast during critical proofs, or mentally compute/assume things without explaining to us. Class structure is very disorganized, chaotic.
Great instructor - makes me want to learn more theory. Infectious enthusiasm, and cool atitude. hard to follow but the material itself was pretty hard.
Great guy. Funny and help. Maybe a little confusing in lecture though
Great Prof. A little off in lecture but so helpful in office hours

Why this man was allowed to teach a class, I will never know. He had no idea what he was talking about (Harvey always corrected him), and attendance dropped dramatically once he started lecturing. He was completely useless and made the class much more confusing than it had to be.

He taught 61A with Brian Harvey, although it would have been better if he didn't in my opinion. While I'm sure he's a good teacher in whatever subject he originally taught, he teach the 61A material effectively and when he taught I would have to go and watch Harvey's old web-casted lectures.
Wasn't very clear at the start of the year, but got better as the year went on. Was never very sure about the material he was teaching, which made lectures hard to understand.

Co-taught CS61A with Harvey, but I really wish Rao didn't. Many times he was confused about how something worked and Harvey (sitting in the class) would have to correct him. Very dull and boring lecturer in general, and I ended up watching Harvey's old lectures.
Not a particularly good lecturer. Seemed confused about how Scheme worked. Often corrected by Harvey. I would recommend webcasting Harvey's old lectures instead of attending lecture.
Lectures jumped all over the place. He simply could not explain a proof clearly, which is a big problem in a proof-based course.
Brilliant Professor. Follows the textbook closely. Helpful in Office Hoursl

Of all my 4 lecturers this year, Rao is by far the most engaging. He integrates occasional jokes into his talking, to which many people seem to take well. Sometimes he fails to explain the connections a particular topic has, and other times he simply assumes we know too much, but overall great prof.

His lectures are so well-thought out and give you a true understanding of deep neural networks. He doesn't just regurgitate formulas/textbooks. He cares about relating topics to real-world research, and the final project really helps with that. The final exam was quite long, but it's not difficult if you honestly complete all the homeworks.

Love how unapologetically academic with is. With limited class resources he made the class great. As a junior with bad enrollment time I wasn't gonna get in, but he took as many people as he could and I did. I love the hands off HW = 0% of grade approach; HW is usually just grade padding for mediocre people and wastes the course staff time to grade
Best lecturer at berkeley but also has a weirdly organized class with a dubious grading scheme

He just struck me as a man who is unhappy with the status he's achieved in life. He was looking to belittle people around him to lift his self-image. It's a shame those people are his students. Created an environment of anxiety and hesitation to ask questions when confused. I've seen him actively shame curious students for not knowing things.
HWs and labs took 20+ hours per week
The lectures were completely irrelevant to the midterm and final, and homework with 20+ sub questions took over 25 hours to complete. Moreover, the labs took loads of time as well.

coming in, was super scared of him, but coming out, I realized he's actually a really nice guy and cares about student learning a lot more than other teachers, despite his reputation. lectures were one of the best i've had at Cal. Highly recommend

nonsensical workload, which champions sacrificing one's time to work at a graduate level in an undergraduate degree. the depth at which he teaches is stimulating, but the testing and assignment workload reeks of someone removed from psycho-social reality -- of the collegiate education system & healthy mentalities not gripped by einstellung...
Gave infinite extra credit so your grade was what you put in. Homeworks were long but fair and doable with OH.
For a junior student, the course is slightly difficult, and it needs a good basis for mathematics and artificial intelligence. Recommend this course strongly!

his 182 is like an EECS grad school tour which individually can be great or terrible. Very approachable during OH and not as scary as people claimed. The only thing I don't like is OH is very limited as he pours out many technical details into the course.

Prof. Sahai is a good lecturer, but that doesn't make up the administrative and content mishaps that CS182 suffers. The contents are focused too much on NLP and not enough on CV and RL, a large portion of the finals were dedicated to one optional homework question at the end, and some TAs don't know what's happening. Many improvements are needed.

Pros: - resubmission policy for homework (largely effort based) - explains things clearly in lecture Cons: - exams do not represent material you learn - GSIs are often unaware of what is going on and it's clear they are reading the solutions from a script - conflicting course policies (huge error in HW calculations at end) - insane workload

Sahai was hard, but in retrospect when I look back at how he told me his goal with 16AB is "to teach you how to think" and look at how the intuition and maturity from 16AB 70 126 and 127 have professionally & philosophically changed my life, it's clear that Sahai is a legend, hence his haters. Haters are just non-optimally envy-driven.
sahai has clear lectures and fair exams. If you attend lecture, discussion, and do the hw with lots of effort, you're gonna learn a lot.

The main problem is that he's really bad at communication, especially regarding non-academic stuff like rubrics or course policies. I didn't like the professor and pnp'd the class, even though the topic (cs182) is fascinating.
Excellent professor with his brilliant intelligence, though the homework is very hard and requires a lot of time, it's still a good class teaching and help me know neural network well.

I would not recommend taking CS182 if you care about your social life. Professor Sahai provides an insanely high workload and is all over the place during lectures. Professor Sahai is a fair grader but you will still spend plenty of time on his course.

Professor Sahai designed and taught this course as if his students have infinite time to rummage through the absolute mess of concepts taught in this course. Get ready for a course that has content almost randomly spread across in a way that makes no sense and an unforgiving set of homeworks sure to drag you down to the pits of depression.

Anant is an intellectual guy who cares about the problems he is working on and is quite intelligent. His students are also going to good universities as Profs. And he is very reasonable as a person. His work isn't *too* deep but if that's your taste he is a decent guy to work with.
Great lectures and cares about students. Workload is very high.

probably a good researcher and is a decent lecturer, but his class structure and expectations do not reflect a professor that understands his students. he genuinely thinks that the daily life of a student should consist of studying almost exclusively with short trips to the bathroom and the dining room table to eat. lots of childhood trauma.

He is the worst professor I have ever seen. Pretending to be caring but in reality you know this person doesn't give anything by giving these random useless time-consuming hw. If hug is a 100, he is negative infinity.

His lectures were clear. I hated how long the homeworks were though. I'm an efficient person, but each problem set took up to 10 hours of my week. He gives easy tests, so it seems like he wants good grades and for people to actually understand the material, but I hated being forced to devote so much of my time to material I didn't enjoy.

You love him or you hate him. The homeworks are absurdly long, but more importantly, you WILL understand the material. His lectures are very clear, the exams are straightforward, and he gives the best curves. Sure, you have to put in 30-40% more work, but it's totally worth it. He doesn't deserve the circlejerk he gets.

The homework is ridiculously long, and Sahai seems to justify it by saying that students should spend their entire lives attending to basic human needs, spending an hour a day meditating, and the rest of your time studying. Super annoying. I'm not bitter reviewing because of a bad gradeI did well in the class, I just hated every second of it.

Homework is a bit too long but the exams/grading are fair. Anant's lectures gave me a good intuition behind the detailed course notes, though sometimes I had to rewatch them and learn the basics myself. Anant is intense at times but there's a lot to learn from him and I'm glad I wasn't scared away by popular rumors. Time consuming but interesting.
Stay away from him if you want to keep your peace of mind.

Sahai unnecessarily complicates things in lecture. Homework is unnecessarily long and sometimes redundant. But he does try to ease things during exams by changing the max score, which really helps. For instance, one exam was out of 150 points but he changed the max score to 120, so it's possible to get more than 100%. But avoid him- not worth it.

CS 70 with Professor Sahai was very homework-heavy but with good time management skills, it is very doable and not at all unreasonable. The lectures are good (not great but not bad). Sahai also really cares about his students and during the quarantine, he made very thoughtful posts on Piazza to help the class get through the tough times.

Sahai clearly thinks students should have no life outside of academics and he expects perfect work ethics from everyone. HW were unnecessarily long. His lectures complicates concepts and I find outside sources much easier to understand (his handwriting doesn't help either.) I do well enough to not care but I know people who do. Avoid if possible.

Hes the absolute worst, stay away from him during your undergraduate career. People who like him would do just as well with any other professor, but say that hes the best because of the clout associated with being a Sahai fanboy.

Hes a mediocre lecturer as he has a tendency to unnecessarily complicate the simplest things. His class policies are terrible; there is widespread panic because he suddenly decides that the peace of mind of his students in no longer important. This semester, he threatened students with expulsion in the middle of a pandemic for looking up homework.
Great professor, cares about students and knows the subject inside out

Many people hate him, many people love him. But I think he is the most caring and a great lecturer. It is true that Sahai have lots of hard homework problems... But his lecture are good. I'm highly recommend to take linear algebra before attend any Sahai's course...

More homework than any other professor that has taught this course. Also, he does not fully cover content in lecture and expects a complete understanding of linear algebra, which is NOT a prerequisite for his course.

I don't understand why he feels the need to bring linear algebra into everything!!!! It makes easier concepts very difficult to understand and it's very frustrating, considering linear algebra is not at all a prereq for this class. Homeworks are also incredibly long and impossible to complete. Very bad experience in the class

Attending lectures is important to get a high grade in the class. He is very dedicated to teaching us. He would really explain things to you until you understand it. He is on Piazza 24/7 to help us. Some unexpected extra credit. If you really want to learn something and get a good grade, take his class.

Sahais course wasnt profoundly challenging or inspiring mathematically. My greatest qualm with his teaching strategy was that he expected a workload that no student/working citizen/ persons with a family can achieve. The irony is that he preached a work-centric lifestyle, but he relied on his TAs for the heavy lifting of the course.

This guy very clearly cares about teaching his students, and he's a very good lecturer. However, he's a genius, and he has an extremely hard time understanding when students don't "get" what he's saying. As a result, don't expect helpful answers or tips on homework, and expect homework sets to be extremely long and difficult.

This man clearly cares about teaching his students and is actually pretty fair with grading but has no chill when it comes to homework and course policy. Type of dude to give you hard concepts and absolutely no supplementary material. Cant wait to take more of his classes uwu.
Gives you a lot of homework but it helps reinforces the lecture ideas. His tests are very easy thankfully.

Prof. Sahai is probably one of the smartest faculty member of Berkeley. However, this does not mean he is good at teaching. His approach on the topics is very math heavy, with a lack of real world examples. Homework tends to be ridiculously long and official notes are non-existent. He can be arrogant at times. Challenge-lovers will enjoy this class
He was quoted saying that students should work 80 hours a week, and the work he assigns reflects that.

My most respected professor at Cal. His HW was long, but really helped me a lot. Despite long HW, the curving was nice and you don't need to put much time compared to other courses since HWs prepare you well for exams. His rigorous style is what every EE/CS class should be like at a school like Berkeley.

Literally the best professor I've ever had, and I'm a senior now. He cares about teaching so much, and puts so much thought into it ridiculous amount more than most other profs. 189 was ridiculously hard, but I easily learnt more in this class than in other 5+ classes combined. It was not hard in a dumb wayevery problem was cleverly thought out.

He talked alot, I mean ALOT. He literally spends 20 min to say 2+2 is equal to 4. I think he himself might have had a hard time understanding these subjects and that's why he thinks everything that he is explaining is difficult. He is not a well educated i guess. He also doesn't give good explanation. Don't expect him to teach you anything!Aweful.
Prof. Sahai is the best professor in the entire school.

Extremely disorganized class. Didn't have notes to begin with, solutions release well after homework deadline (and we had to do self-grades so this didn't help s your forget the homework problems and your methods when solutions are released 5 days after homework deadline). Some homework problems aren't relevant to material taught in lectures.
Prob the worst lecturer known to man.
Makes things unnecessarily complicated when they don't have to be.

Lots of difficult hw. However, his tests are very reflective of the hw, so doing them is essentially exam preparation. While he clearly cares about the class, he can sometimes be blind to students' needs and capabilities. His lectures are often very complicated and math/proof-heavy. However, overall, not a bad professor. You will learn a lot.

Fair grading and midterms; biggest complaint is he sometimes is blind to student needs and you have to wade through hours and hours of technical jargon to even begin to understand his lectures. Probably the most confusing and muddied explanations I've ever had in a class at Berkeley. Great coursework but terrible, terrible lectures.

Clearly passionate about the development of the course and the understanding of his students. Also a clear lecturer who expects students to do their part to understand, but is very willing to help with that process.

Professor Sahai is honestly one of the most amazing professors I've ever had. Yes, he does make you work and yes, his problem sets are quite difficult, but you learn a lot from them. He really cares about your learning and he puts an insane amount of effort in teaching. If you want to learn a lot, he's the professor for you.

Sahai is hard, but he's not bad. I think EE16A will fit him like a glove after a few semesters. I think he just needs to loosen up a bit more in terms of difficulty and he'll be about as good as the top professors here. Definitely worth taking a class with him. He'll boost your confidence and intelligence by making you struggle.

While Sahai isn't the worst, he isn't the best either. He knows his stuff, but rest assured, if you don't come into this course having a decent knowledge of linear algebra, it kicks you pretty hard, especially if you're a busy person. Sahai is a bit pretentious and makes it pretty clear he values the strong students, as he was once considered one.
Extremely great professor. His lectures are very clear, and he stresses intuition along with math. Problem sets are difficult, but worth it.
This dude will beat you over the head with math, and make you love it.

Excellent lecturer, take if you want a real good understanding of discrete mathematics and if you want to understand the math that will be fundamental in upper division CS and EE courses. Otherwise if you don't like math or if you are a lazy person, save yourself the trouble and take CS70 with a less rigorous professor.
HORRIBLE LECTURER

Homework will take up most of your time in this class, but the fact that they take so long means that studying (for the midterms, anyway) is kept to a minimum. Read the lecture notes, go to lecture, and go to section.
14-20 hour problem sets and a bunch of other inane tasks. No, they do not help you understand the material better, they just waste your time. His attitude is also rather arrogant and condescending.
Terrible professor. Avoid him at all costs.

Sahai was probably the BEST professor I've had at Berkeley. His class demanded a lot of work but he is extremely invested in his students' success and will go to great lengths to make it happen. Despite the work grading is pretty lenient too with plenty of partial credit to go around. I learned so much in this class and would strongly recommend.

Sahai is great, but class is intense. If you devote a substantial amount of time to CS70, you'll learn a lot and have a great experience (but it can be rather stressful). Be warned, virtual labs were a challenge. Form a study group, go to homework parties - do whatever you need to. And don't cheat or inflate your grades - you will get caught.

Sahai pours his heart and soul into CS70. He is accessible, helpful, and clearly cares about his students' well-being both inside and outside of class. However, he expects the same level of dedication from students. Easily one of the most demanding professors in EECS. If you can devote 25+ hrs/week, an amazing class to take, you'll learn a lot.

Good class material. Lectures were clear for the most part. Class was a bit disorganized as it was a 10-person class the semester I took it and I remembered not getting any feedback on homeworks until the very end. Offers extra credit to boost grade, which helps because his exams are quite challenging.

Take this class with Sahai if you like spending 20 hours every weekend bashing your head against your desk on impossibly difficult problem sets. To his credit he is passionate and puts a lot of effort into it, but is it worth making life miserable for the 90% of students that struggle with the material and for the GSI's? I think not.

If professors received participation grades, this man would easily get an A+. Sahai's p-sets and (to a lesser extent) his midterms are difficult and hit a depth beyond the average student, but he goes above and beyond in providing students with as many resources to succeed as possible. In the end he also grades fairly (and is willing to adjust).

Sahai's teaching style is impeccable. He displays a very clear, nuanced approach, making his lectures rigorous but worthwhile. Problem Sets are challenging, midterms less so, but the class in itself is quite brilliant. Sahai and Papadimitriou together make for a v. enjoyable semester.

Sahai is a good prof. for the probability portion of the experimental CS70-2 discrete math section. He knows many applications off the top of his head and also is always ready with intuitive heuristics to help understand how probability works. He is clear and interested in students learning. Plus I like the green shirts in front of a green board :)
